items.q: What is the reciprocal of the mixed number 2 1/3?
items.correct: 3/7 — first convert to the improper fraction 7/3, then flip it.
items.misconception: 1/2 1/3, by flipping each part of the mixed number separately.
items.why: Students don't convert mixed numbers to improper fractions before taking the reciprocal, and confuse 'reciprocal' with 'negative'.

items.correct: 9 — squaring a negative gives a positive.
items.misconception: (-3)^2 equals -9 because the minus is preserved.
items.random_wrong: (-3)^2 equals -6 because the negative makes you subtract.
items.why: Students confuse (-3)^2 with -3^2; without brackets the second is indeed -9, but with brackets the negative is squared too.

items.correct: 2 — subtracting a negative is the same as adding, so -3 + 5 = 2.
items.misconception: -8, because two negatives combine to make a bigger negative.
items.random_wrong: -15, because two negatives multiply to give a larger negative result.
items.why: Students apply 'two negatives make a positive' inconsistently or fall back on the rule 'minus and minus is more minus' from addition contexts.
Correct answer: π. “0.5” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: 9. “11” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The real figure is 36. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
